disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The behavioural escalation began right after a change to routine — a community outing was delayed about two hours and the patient appears to have taken the delay as a permanent cancellation
trigger_question_examples: What was happening just before he became upset?
Did anything change in his routine or plans today?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: His outing got pushed back about two hours because of transport, and he seems to have decided it was cancelled for good. That's when it all started.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The patient has a long-standing severe intellectual disability with difficulty processing abstract or temporal concepts, and normally needs daily support for emotional regulation
trigger_question_examples: Can you tell me about his usual health and support needs?
Does he have any conditions I should know about?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He has a severe intellectual disability, he's had it since he was little, and he needs a lot of help every day — especially managing his feelings. He can't really understand ideas like 'later'.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: There is ongoing property destruction and self-injury — he is throwing and breaking objects and has given himself superficial forearm scratches; broken crockery and glass are on the floor
trigger_question_examples: Is he hurting himself or breaking things?
Are there any sharp or broken objects near him?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's smashing things and there's broken crockery everywhere. He's scratched up his own forearms throwing stuff, though they look like surface scratches.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: He is not responding to verbal reassurance or redirection at all, and the episode has continued for about three hours — far longer and more intense than his usual upsets
trigger_question_examples: Is anything you try helping to calm him?
How long has this been going on and is it easing?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Nothing is working. I've tried everything and he won't respond to me. It's been about three hours and it's not settling — that's not like him.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Screen for an occult physical driver of the behaviour change — no fever or signs of illness, bowels opening normally and no constipation reported, no obvious tooth or ear pain, and no urinary problems reported by staff
trigger_question_examples: Could he be in pain somewhere — tummy, teeth, ears, or trouble passing urine?
Has he been unwell, feverish, or constipated recently?
When did he last open his bowels or eat and drink normally?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Now that you ask — no, he's not been sick or feverish, his bowels have been normal, he's not been holding his face or ear like he's in pain, and he's been passing urine fine as far as I know.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: I hadn't really thought about him being in pain — I just assumed it was the outing being cancelled.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No preceding blackout, collapse, stiffening/jerking, or loss of bladder control before the behaviour started
trigger_question_examples: Before this began, did he black out, collapse, or have any shaking or stiffening?
Any loss of bladder or bowel control before it started?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, there was nothing like that — no collapse, no shaking or stiffening, no wetting himself. He just got more and more upset after the outing was delayed.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: He takes a regular daily SSRI and has no as-needed sedating or antipsychotic medication charted for home use
trigger_question_examples: What medicines does he take, and is there anything as-needed for agitation?
Is there a PRN calming medication in his plan?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's on his usual daily antidepressant. There's nothing 'as needed' charted for when he gets like this — we don't have a calming medication we're allowed to give him.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The aggression is currently directed at objects rather than people, but there is a real risk it could turn toward staff, and there is a risk he could try to pick up or swallow broken pieces
trigger_question_examples: Is he directing any of this at you or other people?
Is there any chance he could pick up or swallow the broken pieces?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: So far it's the furniture and crockery, not me — but I'm worried it could turn on me, and there's broken glass around that he could grab.

patient_initiated_exchanges.trigger_condition: If the AI Doctor pauses or gives only general reassurance without a plan
patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: I'm on my own here and he's getting worse — please tell me what to actually do right now.
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Acknowledges the immediate safety risk, gives concrete de-escalation and environmental-safety steps, and moves promptly to arranging an urgent in-person response rather than attempting to manage the crisis remotely.

deflection_behaviours.description: The support worker attributes everything to the cancelled outing and may resist screening for a physical cause.
deflection_behaviours.trigger_condition: When first asked whether the patient could be in pain or unwell
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: It's definitely just the outing — he does this when his routine changes.
